title: The Conscience and the Law
speaker: Pastor Dennis Leaman
source_files: 11-17-24 -AM- The Conscience and the Law - Hebrews 9_1-14 - Pastor Dennis Leaman.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Conscience and Existentialism
subpoints: R.C. Sproul preferred Hebrews over Romans because it brings both covenants together
Kant marveled at two things: the heavens and the conscience in man — that is where the people in the pew are
Existentialism (no God, make your own essence by your choices) fails because without God, there is no moral difference between good and evil choices
The conscience tied to the law of God is a three-strand rope, but it becomes an unrelenting burden without Christ

outline.title: 1. The Old Covenant: Glorious but Passing
subpoints: The tabernacle structure: the outer sanctuary and the Holy of Holies — the high priest alone entered once a year with blood for himself and the people
The Holy Spirit indicated that the way into the holiest of all was not yet revealed while the first tabernacle stood (Heb 9:8)
The old covenant was the ministry of death — written on stones; Moses's face shone so gloriously he had to veil it, but the glory was passing away
2 Corinthians 3: if the ministry of death was glorious, how much more glorious is the new covenant of the spirit that gives life

outline.title: 2. Christ the High Priest of the New Covenant
subpoints: Christ came as high priest of the good things to come with a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands
Not with the blood of goats and calves but with his own blood he entered the Most Holy Place once for all, having obtained eternal redemption
The blood of bulls and goats purified the flesh; how much more shall the blood of Christ cleanse your conscience from dead works to serve the living God
The one great cry of the passage: put it to sleep — the guilt of conscience under the law is finished in Christ

outline.title: 3. The Law of Faith — The New Regime
subpoints: Romans 3:20 — by the deeds of the law no flesh is justified; the law gives knowledge of sin
"But now" — the greatest conjunction of Scripture — the righteousness of God apart from the law is revealed, through faith in Jesus Christ
Romans 3:27 — boasting is excluded, not by the law of works, but by the law of faith
Faith in the finished work of Christ — not faith in faith, but historically grounded, objectively based faith — is the only thing that can put the conscience to rest
Illustration: a young man cutting himself in prison; a teenage girl cutting herself in Vancouver — Christ has already been cut for us; we do not need to punish ourselves

summary: Pastor Dennis Leaman, an 84-year-old elder preaching in Pastor Lyro's absence, opens with a vivid meditation on the conscience and its relationship to the law, drawing on Immanuel Kant's two great marvels — the starry heavens and the moral conscience. He argues that the conscience tied to the law of God becomes an unbearable burden without Christ. Moving through Hebrews 9:1-14, he traces the structure of the old covenant — the tabernacle, the annual entry of the high priest into the Holy of Holies with blood — and shows that it was glorious but intentionally temporary, a ministry of death meant to slay sinners so that the new covenant could give life. Drawing on 2 Corinthians 3, he shows that Moses's fading glory prefigured the passing of the old system. The great turn of the passage is Christ entering the true heavenly sanctuary once for all with his own blood, obtaining eternal redemption and cleansing the conscience from dead works. In Romans 3, the law of works is replaced by the law of faith — boasting is excluded, and the righteousness of God through faith in Christ is revealed apart from the law. The sermon closes with two pastoral illustrations of self-harm driven by a conscience the law has wounded, urging the congregation to put that guilt to rest in Christ who has already borne the punishment.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Conscience and the Law

R.C. Sproul said that if he had to go to prison and could only take one book, it would not be Romans — he said he had already memorized Romans. He would take Hebrews, because Hebrews brings the old covenant and the new covenant together.

I want to talk to you about the old covenant and the new covenant this morning. Let me say that I am thankful for the opportunity to speak. I am 84 years old and losing some of my abilities — but I know the Lord is going to shine through.

I want to get the young people's attention. I want to put something to rest for you today. Emmanuel Kant said there were two things that made him marvel: the heavens when he looked at the stars, and the conscience in man. I told the men at the seminary — that is where your people are. In the conscience. It always works. You wake up at three in the morning and your conscience is speaking to you about what you have done. I would like to go back to age 13 and live my life over. My conscience never forgets.

Sproul said that conscience is a great moral good in the world — there would be much more mischief and sin without it. We need to listen to the conscience. But if you follow your conscience without the law of God, you can be in great trouble. I visited the Pocus Nova Indians in the heart of the Amazon — cannibals 150 years ago. Their conscience did not bother them. You need the conscience tied to the law of God.

So we have the law of God. And we have our conscience. And we tie these together with Scripture. A three-strand rope is hard to break. But what do we do with that nagging conscience that will not let us forget?

## 1. The Old Covenant: Glorious but Passing

Let me turn to my text. Hebrews chapter 9. The tabernacle was prepared — the outer sanctuary with the lampstand, the table, and the showbread. And behind the second veil, the holiest of all, with the golden altar, the ark of the covenant, the golden pot of manna, Aaron's rod, the tablets of the covenant, and the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat.

The priests always entered the first part performing their services. But into the second part, the high priest went alone, once a year, not without blood which he offered for himself and for the sins of the people. The Holy Spirit was indicating that the way into the holiest of all was not yet revealed while the first tabernacle was still standing.

Now turn with me to 2 Corinthians chapter 3. Our sufficiency is from God, who made us sufficient as ministers of the new covenant — not of the letter, but of the Spirit. For the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life. If the ministry of death, written and engraved in stones, was glorious — so glorious that the children of Israel could not look steadfastly upon the face of Moses because of the glory of his countenance — yet that glory was passing away.

The old covenant with the emphasis on the law was passing away. It was glorious. It was efficacious. It did what it was supposed to do: it slew you. But so many Christians are still in that estate — slain by their conscience under the law. I am crying out to you: that law and that conscience under the law is put to rest in Christ. It was passing away. Moses was not allowed to enter the Promised Land. That old system was a system of death.

## 2. Christ the High Priest of the New Covenant

But Christ came as a high priest of the good things to come, with a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is, not of this creation. Not with the blood of goats and calves, but with his own blood he entered the Most Holy Place once for all — you hear that? Once for all — having obtained eternal redemption.

For if the blood of bulls and goats and the ashes of a heifer, sprinkling the unclean, sanctified to the purifying of the flesh — how much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God, cleanse your conscience from dead works to serve the living God?

Put it to sleep. That is what he is saying. That is the old economy that had to go to sleep with Moses. We have a new program. And it is all about Jesus. All about forgiveness. No matter what you have done.

If God can forgive David — Psalm 51, "Have mercy upon me, O Lord, according to your lovingkindness; blot out my transgressions" — there is only one place for that nagging conscience: in the arms of the Lord Jesus Christ.

## 3. The Law of Faith

You say, "Pastor, I know that law and I know my conscience and I know what has happened to me and I know my sin." My friends, you are under the wrong law.

Romans chapter 3. By the deeds of the law no flesh will be justified in his sight, for by the law is the knowledge of sin. But now — the greatest conjunction of all of Scripture — the righteousness of God, apart from the law, is revealed, being witnessed by the law and the prophets. What is that? Even the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ.

Where is boasting then? It is excluded. By what law? Of works? No. But by the law of faith.

Faith in the finished work of the Lord Jesus Christ. Not faith in faith — but a historical, redemptive, objective theology. Christ died for our sins. He is the object of our worship. Not just an example for us — he is the object of our worship. And that, and only that, is going to ease your conscience.

I had to visit a young man in prison who was cutting himself because his conscience was unbearable. I told him: do you not know that Isaiah says Christ was bruised for our iniquities? The chastisement for our peace was upon him, and with his stripes we are healed. In Vancouver, a thirteen-year-old girl was cutting herself because of her conscience. After I preached about this, she stood at a distance and waited. I should have gone to her. I should have held her.

Friends, if you are thinking about cutting yourself, or worse — Christ has already been cut for you. That punishment has already taken place in the Lord Jesus Christ. Put it to rest. And have faith — not faith in faith, but faith in the finished work of the Lord Jesus Christ.
